Input Text: Large language models have shown strong performance in text generation, but retrieval-augmented generation remains challenging. This work applies structured prompting to improve reliability and reduce errors in generated scientific summaries. The results indicate that the method improves phrase-level overlap with reference summaries. A remaining limitation is that long inputs require truncation.
Reference Summary: The paper investigates retrieval-augmented generation with structured prompting and finds that it improves phrase-level overlap with reference summaries, although long inputs require truncation.
LoRA Output: This work uses structured prompting to improve retrieval-augmented generation, showing that it improves phrase-level overlap with reference summaries, and shows that long inputs require truncation.

Input Text: This research investigates how human-in-the-loop error analysis can support domain adaptation. The system is evaluated using summarization metrics and qualitative analysis of generated outputs. Results demonstrate that the method improves alignment between generated and reference summaries. The main limitation is that performance remains limited by dataset size.
Reference Summary: This work applies human-in-the-loop error analysis to domain adaptation, demonstrating improved summarization behavior despite the limitation that performance remains limited by dataset size.

Input Text: Scientific documents are difficult to summarize because they contain dense technical information and complex claims. This study focuses on document-level generation and evaluates attention-based sequence-to-sequence modeling as a practical solution. Findings suggest that the proposed approach reduces hallucinated claims. Despite these gains, long inputs require truncation.
Reference Summary: This study uses attention-based sequence-to-sequence modeling to improve document-level generation, showing that the approach reduces hallucinated claims while noting that long inputs require truncation.
LoRA Output: This study uses attention-based sequence-to-sequence modeling to reduce hallucinated claims, showing that the approach reduces hallucinous claims while still requiring truncation.
